paragraphs.text: Concerning the few possessions taken by
the last surviving native of San Nicolas Island

in 1853, published records are scarce or non-existent. The basketry analysis presented here is based upon a photograph, reproduced herewith, of a San Nicolas Island twined specimen which finally came to the
, but which was destroyed in the fire following the 1906 earthquake.
the last survivors of the Nicoleño tribe

where, after four months, she died. The several published and manuscript accounts of her life alone for eighteen years on

The few personal possessions which she brought with her to
in 1853 are said to have been sent to
except for one piece described as a "water bottle made of rushes and covered with asphaltum." This water bottle was reported in 1904 by
as in the possession of the
, and the illustrations of that same basketry piece shown here (Fig. 1) are reproduced from photographic prints found in the
. The photographs were secured by Merriam's efforts, and are of particular value because the basket was destroyed, along with the building and other collections of
, in the fire following the 1906 earthquake.

Dimensions of the bottle are not recorded, but its height may be estimated at about 10 inches (ca. 25 cm.) and its diameter at about 6 inches (ca. 16 cm.). The base of the bottle has a surface coating of asphaltum and there are small spots randomly distrubuted on the outer surface of the basket which apparently are daubs of asphaltum applied over leaks. Since

is known to have followed the customary Chumash-Gabrielino trick of coating the interior of baskets with asphaltum by means of lumps of asphalt and hot stones which melted the asphalt and distrubed it over the inner walls, it seems unlikely that the basket was, as
The basket is made by the twined technique and the accompanying diagram (Fig. 2a) of technique has been kindly provided by

. In the diagram the dotted lines represent weft elements which have disappeared through rotting away. The disintegrated wefts may have been of buckskin, fur strips, feathers or hair. Two main areas, the body just above the center, and the neck, once held the decorative bands which have now disappeared. The ordinary warp appears to be of bundles of fibers rather than a single vegetal element, and this type of warp according to

, who has made a particular study of Southern California basketry, is also the ordinary Chumash technique. The alternately spaced wefts, now disappeared, probably were decorative elements. The alternating pitch of single pairs of weft elements to form a chevron has not been noted by
as a Chumash basketry trait, but he has observed this in two baskets attributed to Salinan manufacture, and this feature has been also noted by
on some of the basketry fragments from
Beyond this not much can be said. Any basket from the
is of interest because we know so little about the textile art of such groups as the

specimen lies partly in the circumstances surrounding its history and preservation of this bare record in the form of a photograph now over half a century old. That such water bottles were commonly used by the
seems probable since the mainland desert groups are otherwise reported to have used such forms, and there is a
record for a large asphalt-sealed water bottle of basketry (
, 1955, p. [102]) as well as one for the
(ibid., p. 163). That there remain as yet undiscovered baskets dating from the eighteenth century is a practical certainty, and in the course of time museum collections in
will probably yield such treasures to the seeker.
on the Anza expedition in 1776 says that he saw no baskets being made on the
coast and quotes some religious colleagues who told him that baskets were made on
and traded from there to the mainland people. Anza collected a number of baskets from the
on the way north with the intention of taking them back to
, and it is these pieces which may ultimately turn up in
if a search is made for them.

remarked on the return trip a few months later that no baskets were to be seen in the Channel villages because the expedition had already collected them--presumably the supply was not yet replenished. While speaking of unusual baskets, reference should be made to the two remarkable early ninteenth century specimens, one of which is now in the
University of California Robert H. Lowie Museum of Anthropology
